title: Rotate the secret you already deleted: detect-secrets and the key still living in git history
brief: Scan a repo with `detect-secrets scan`, then face the part nobody likes: deleting the line and committing does not remove the key — it sits in every prior commit and was scraped seconds after you pushed. The real fix is rotate first, then purge history with git filter-repo, plus a pre-commit hook so it never lands again. The failure — 'I deleted it, we're fine' — stays in.
source_title: Secure Coding: Preventing the OWASP Top 10
rationale: it-journey teaches secret handling straight; lifehacker has git and .gitignore hacks but nothing on scanning committed secrets or why deletion is theater. The 'you deleted it and it's still there' gotcha is real, testable, and on-voice.

title: pip-audit: the dependency scanner that finds real holes and a pile you can't patch
brief: Honest verdict on `pip-audit` (and its npm audit cousin): it catches genuinely known-vulnerable versions in seconds, and it also floods you with transitive-dependency advisories you can't fix and scores that don't match your actual risk. What earns an upgrade, what's noise, and when to wire it into CI versus running it by hand.
source_title: Secure Coding: Preventing the OWASP Top 10
rationale: The tools lane is all coreutils rewrites (rg/fd/bat/eza...); a dependency-audit scanner is fresh territory and it-journey names pip-audit/npm audit as the supply-chain check. The honest angle — most of what it reports you can't act on — is exactly this pillar's promise.

title: Scope the token before your MCP server gets the keys: a fine-grained PAT that can't touch prod
brief: An MCP server inherits every permission of the token you hand it, so a classic admin PAT means one bad prompt can force-push. Build a fine-grained personal access token with read-only Contents/Issues/PR scopes, wire it into the server's env, and prove the limit by asking the agent to do something it shouldn't and watching it get a 403. The gotcha: the easy token is the dangerous one.
rationale: lifehacker's meta docs cover its own autopilot but there's no general hack on scoping the token an MCP/agent runs under. Fine-grained PAT scoping is concrete, testable, and the least-privilege footgun is a real, on-voice failure.

title: STRIDE your side project in 20 minutes: one data flow, six questions, a ranked fix list
brief: Skip the enterprise threat-model binder. Draw one data-flow diagram (you, your app, your database), run the six STRIDE questions against the single arrow that crosses a trust boundary, and rank what you find by likelihood times impact. You'll know it worked when you have three fixes worth doing this week — and the honest part is that the paper version beats the fancy modeling tool you'll never open twice.
rationale: it-journey teaches STRIDE/DFDs earnestly; lifehacker has no security-process content, and the 'do the 20-minute version on one flow' angle is genuinely useful while poking at security theater — satire on top of a real method.

title: Scope your GitHub Actions permissions block before the default token can write to everything
brief: The default GITHUB_TOKEN hands a workflow broad write access to your whole repo, so one compromised action can push to main. Add a top-level `permissions:` block set to `contents: read`, then grant each job only the scope it truly needs. The footgun that stays in: the moment you declare that block, every scope you didn't list drops to no-access, so the job that pushes a tag or cuts a release starts failing with a 403 until you add `contents: write` back exactly where it belongs.
rationale: it-journey teaches least-privilege workflow permissions as agent governance; lifehacker's Actions hacks cover GH_TOKEN and packaging workflows but never scoping the permissions block itself. The 'lock it down and now the release job 403s' gotcha is real, testable, and on-voice.

title: type -a before you alias: see what a command name already runs so you don't shadow it
brief: Before you drop `alias ls='ls --color'` into your .bashrc, run `type -a ls` to see everything that name already resolves to — alias, shell builtin, function, and the real /bin/ls, in the order the shell picks them. The failure that stays in: you alias over a name a script depends on, it breaks in a way that works fine when you paste the same command by hand, and `command ls` (which skips the alias) is the one-word escape hatch.
rationale: The cellar quest teaches alias/unalias/type earnestly; lifehacker has a git-alias pack and mentions shadowing a builtin once, but nothing on checking what a name resolves to first. type -a / command is offline-testable and the shadow-a-name footgun is on-voice.

title: Read a failing CI run from your terminal: gh run view --log-failed and the watch --exit-status trap
brief: Stop clicking through the Actions web UI to find why a run went red. `gh run view --log-failed` prints just the failing step's log, and `gh run watch` follows a run live. The trap that stays in: `gh run watch` exits 0 even when the run failed, so a script that gates on it treats a broken build as green — you have to add `--exit-status` to make the failure actually propagate.
rationale: it-journey lists gh run list/view as the machine-verifiable CI signal; lifehacker's gh hack covers setting GH_TOKEN, not reading results. The --exit-status footgun — a watch that returns success on a failed run — is a real, testable, on-voice gotcha nothing else covers.

title: chmod 644 and 755, never 777: the permission bits that decide what's safe to run
brief: Read an `ls -l` line as a file-type char plus three rwx triples, then set them on purpose: 644 (rw-r--r--) for data, 755 (rwxr-xr-x) for scripts, and never 777, which hands write access to everyone on the box. Two failures stay in: you `chmod +x` a script that has no `#!` shebang line and it still won't run, and you forget the `./` prefix because the current directory isn't on your PATH.
rationale: The armoury quest teaches chmod and permission bits earnestly; lifehacker has ssh/tar/rsync/xargs hacks but no chmod hack at all. Octal-vs-symbolic notation, the 777 security footgun, and the shebang/PATH failures are all offline-testable and on-voice.
